title: Sparse Mixture Routing with Learned Temperature for Efficient Inference
abstract: We introduce a learned-temperature gating mechanism for sparse mixture-of-experts layers that adapts routing sharpness per token. On three language-modeling benchmarks the method matches dense-model quality at 38% of the active FLOPs, and we provide ablations isolating the temperature schedule, expert count, and load-balancing loss. Code and training configs are released.
excerpt: Section 4 reports results on WikiText-103, C4, and The Pile, each averaged over 3 seeds with 95% confidence intervals. Table 2 ablates the temperature schedule against a fixed-temperature baseline and a no-gating dense model matched for parameter count. Section 5 analyses routing entropy over training and shows the learned temperature anneals automatically. Appendix B gives full hyperparameters and the load-balancing coefficient sweep.

review.summary: The paper proposes a per-token learned-temperature gate for sparse mixture-of-experts layers and shows it preserves dense-model quality at substantially reduced active compute, with ablations isolating each component.
strengths: Clear, well-motivated mechanism with a sensible adaptive-sharpness intuition.
Experiments span three datasets with multiple seeds and confidence intervals; the FLOP-matched dense baseline is a fair comparison.
Ablations isolate the temperature schedule, expert count, and load-balancing loss, supporting the central claim.
Code and configs released, aiding reproducibility.
weaknesses: No comparison against recent learned-routing baselines beyond fixed-temperature gating.
Wall-clock latency, not just FLOPs, would strengthen the efficiency claim.
questions: How sensitive is the method to the load-balancing coefficient outside the swept range?
Does the learned temperature transfer across model scales without retuning?
review.limitations: Limitations around scale and latency are acknowledged briefly; ethical concerns are minimal for this work and adequately noted.

title: A Unified Framework for Graph Augmentation via Random Edge Perturbation
abstract: We present a framework that improves graph neural network accuracy by randomly perturbing edges during training. Across several node-classification datasets our method increases accuracy. We argue this constitutes a general principle for graph augmentation.
excerpt: Section 3 evaluates on Cora, Citeseer, and PubMed using a single train/test split and reports accuracy without standard deviations. The proposed method is compared only to a no-augmentation GCN baseline; DropEdge, GraphCL, and other standard augmentation baselines are mentioned in related work but not run. Section 4 claims generality but provides no theoretical analysis and no experiments on larger or heterophilous graphs. Hyperparameters are not reported.

review.summary: The paper proposes random edge perturbation as a graph-augmentation method and reports accuracy gains on three small citation benchmarks, claiming a general augmentation principle.
strengths: The idea is simple and easy to implement.
The writing in the introduction is accessible.
weaknesses: Evaluation uses a single split with no standard deviations, so reported gains may be within noise.
Directly related baselines (DropEdge, GraphCL) are discussed but never compared against empirically, despite being the obvious points of contrast.
The generality claim is unsupported: no theory and no experiments beyond three small homophilous citation graphs.
Hyperparameters and tuning protocol are not reported, harming reproducibility.
questions: How does the method compare to DropEdge under an identical training budget and multiple seeds?
Do the gains persist on larger or heterophilous graphs?
review.limitations: Limitations are not meaningfully discussed; the generality claim is not scoped honestly to the narrow evaluation.
